DRAFT FOR SECOND CONSULTATION 
Depression in adults: treatment and management: NICE guideline short version DRAFT 
(May 2018) 
1 of 76 
Depression in adults: treatment and 
1 
management 
2 
 
3 
NICE guideline: short version 
4 
Draft for second consultation, May 2018 
5 
 
6 
This guideline covers identifying, treating and managing depression in people 
aged 18 and over. It recommends tailoring care and treatment based on the 
severity of a person’s depression. It also includes advice on preventing relapse 
and managing complex and severe depression. 
Who is it for? 
 Healthcare professionals 
 Other professionals who have direct contact with, or provide health and other 
public services for, people with depression 
 Commissioners and providers of services for people with depression and their 
families and carers 
 Adults with depression, their families and carers 
This guideline will update and replace NICE guideline CG90 (published October 
2009). 
We have updated or added new recommendations on the treatment of new 
depressive episodes, further treatment, treatment of chronic, psychotic and 
complex depression, preventing relapse and the organisation of and access to 
services. 
You are invited to comment on the new and updated recommendations in this 
guideline. These are marked as:

DRAFT FOR SECOND CONSULTATION 
Depression in adults: treatment and management: NICE guideline short version DRAFT 
(May 2018) 
2 of 76 
 [new 2018] if the evidence has been reviewed and the recommendation has 
been added or updated or 
 [2018] if the evidence has been reviewed but no change has been made to the 
recommended action. 
You are also invited to comment on recommendations that NICE proposes to 
delete from the 2009 guideline. 
We have not updated recommendations shaded in grey, and cannot accept 
comments on them. In some cases, we have made minor wording changes for 
clarification. 
See Update information for a full explanation of what is being updated. 
This version of the guideline contains the draft recommendations, context and 
recommendations for research. Information about how the guideline was 
developed is on the guideline’s page on the NICE website. This includes the 
guideline committee’s discussion and the evidence reviews (in the full guideline), 
the scope, and details of the committee and any declarations of interest.

Recommendations

People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed 
decisions about their care, as described in your care. 
Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show 
the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about 
prescribing medicines (including off-label use), professional guidelines, 
standards and laws (including on consent and mental capacity), and 
safeguarding.

Depression severity 
1 
In this guideline the terms ‘less severe depression’ and ‘more severe depression’ are 
2 
used. Depression severity exists along a continuum and is essentially composed of 
3 
three elements:  
4 
 symptoms (which may vary in frequency and intensity) 
5 
 duration of the disorder 
6 
  the impact on personal and social functioning. 
7 
Severity of depression is therefore a consequence of the contribution of all of these 
8 
elements. 
9 
Traditionally, depression severity has been grouped under 4 categories: 
10 
 ‘severe depression’, characterised by a large number of symptoms with a major 
11 
negative impact on personal and social functioning 
12 
  ‘moderate depression’, which has a smaller number of symptoms with a more 
13 
limited negative impact on personal and social functioning 
14 
  ‘mild depression’, which has a small number of symptoms with a limited impact 
15 
on personal and social functioning, and 
16 
 ‘sub-threshold depressive symptoms’, which do not meet criteria for a diagnosis of 
17 
depression and which typically have little impact on personal and social 
18 
functioning. 
19 
In the development of the recommendations for this guideline the committee wanted 
20 
to develop a way of representing the severity of depression in the recommendations 
21 
which best represents the available evidence on the classification and would help the 
22 
uptake of the recommendations in routine clinical practice. They therefore decided to 
23 
use the terms ‘less severe depression’, which includes the traditional categories of 
24 
subthreshold symptoms, mild depression, and the lower half of moderate 
25 
depression; and ‘more severe depression’, which includes the traditional categories 
26 
of the upper half of moderate depression and severe depression.  
27

(May 2018) 
42 of 76 
Assessment of depression 
1 
This is based on the criteria in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual, 5th edition 
2 
(DSM-5). Assessment should include the number and severity of symptoms, duration 
3 
of the current episode, and course of illness.  
4 
Key symptoms:  
5 
 persistent sadness or low mood; and/or 
6 
 marked loss of interests or pleasure. 
7 
At least one of these, most days, most of the time for at least 2 weeks. 
8 
If any of above present, ask about associated symptoms:  
9 
 disturbed sleep (decreased or increased compared to usual) 
10 
 decreased or increased appetite and/or weight 
11 
 fatigue or loss of energy 
12 
 agitation or slowing of movements 
13 
 poor concentration or indecisiveness 
14 
 feelings of worthlessness or excessive or inappropriate guilt  
15 
 suicidal thoughts or acts. 
16 
The Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ-9, Kroenke et al., 2001) asks about these 
17 
nine DSM symptoms, and can be used to count symptoms, but the number of 
18 
symptoms is not sufficient in itself to define severity, as patients vary in their 
19 
readiness to volunteer symptoms. Further enquiry is essential.  
20 
Then, ask about duration and associated disability, past and family history of mood 
21 
disorders, and availability of social support.  
22 
Less severe depression 
23 
This is typically depression with fewer than 7 symptoms and minor or moderate 
24 
functional impairment. 
25

DRAFT FOR SECOND CONSULTATION 
Depression in adults: treatment and management: NICE guideline short version DRAFT 
(May 2018) 
43 of 76 
More severe depression 
1 
This is typically depression with 7 or more symptoms and moderate to severe 
2 
functional impairment. 
3 
Factors that favour general advice and active monitoring 
4 
 four or fewer of the above symptoms with little associated disability (‘sub-threshold 
5 
depression’) 
6 
 symptoms intermittent, or less than 2 weeks' duration 
7 
 recent onset with identified stressor 
8 
 no past or family history of depression 
9 
 social support available 
10 
 lack of suicidal thoughts. 
11 
Active monitoring can be aided by use of a validated questionnaire such as the PHQ-
12 
9, as it has been shown to be sensitive to change at the individual patient level 
13 
(Lowe et al., 2004). 
14 
Factors that favour more active treatment in primary care 
15 
 five or more symptoms with associated disability 
16 
 persistent or long-standing symptoms 
17 
 personal or family history of depression 
18 
 low social support 
19 
 occasional suicidal thoughts. 
20 
Factors that favour referral to mental health professionals 
21 
 inadequate or incomplete response to two or more interventions 
22 
 recurrent episode within 1 year of last one  
23 
 history suggestive of bipolar disorder 
24 
 the person with depression or relatives request referral 
25 
 more persistent suicidal thoughts 
26 
 self-neglect. 
27 
Factors that favour urgent referral to specialist mental health services 
28 
 actively suicidal ideas or plans 
29

References 
4 
Kroenke K, Spitzer RL, Williams JB. The PHQ-9: validity of a brief depression 
5 
measure. J Gen Intern Med 2001, 16:606-613. 
6 
Löwe B, Kroenke K, Herzog W Grafe K. Measuring depression outcome with a brief 
7 
self-report instrument: sensitivity to change of the Patient Health Questionnaire 
8 
(PHQ-9). J Affective Dis 81 (2004) 61–66. 
9 
Medication management 
10 
Medication management is giving a person advice on how to keep to a regime for 
11 
the use of medication (for example, how to take it, when to take it and how often). 
12 
The focus in such programmes is only on the management of medication and not on 
13 
other aspects of depression. 
14 
Routine (sessional) outcome monitoring 
15 
This is a system for the monitoring of the outcomes of treatments which involves 
16 
regular (usually at each contact: referred to as sessional) assessment of symptoms 
17 
or functioning using a valid scale. It can inform both service user and practitioner of 
18 
progress in treatment. It is often supported by computerised delivery and scoring of 
19 
the measures which ensures better completion of the questionnaires and service 
20 
level audit and evaluation.  Alternative terms such as “sessional outcome monitoring” 
21 
or sessional outcomes” may also be used which emphasise that outcomes should be 
22 
recorded at each contact.  
23 
Stepped care 
24 
This is a system of delivering and monitoring treatments, so that the most effective, 
25 
least intrusive and least resource intensive treatments are delivered first. Stepped 
26 
care has a built in ‘self-correcting’ mechanism so that people who do not benefit from 
27 
initial interventions can be ‘stepped up’ to more intensive interventions as needed. 
28

needed. Implementing change is most effective when aligned with local priorities. 
12 
Changes recommended for clinical practice that can be done quickly – like changes 
13 
in prescribing practice – should be shared quickly. This is because healthcare 
14 
professionals should use guidelines to guide their work – as is required by 
15 
professional regulating bodies such as the General Medical and Nursing and 
16 
Midwifery Councils. 
17 
Changes should be implemented as soon as possible, unless there is a good reason 
18 
for not doing so (for example, if it would be better value for money if a package of 
19 
recommendations were all implemented at once). 
20 
Different organisations may need different approaches to implementation, depending 
21 
on their size and function. Sometimes individual practitioners may be able to respond 
22 
to recommendations to improve their practice more quickly than large organisations. 
23 
Here are some pointers to help organisations put NICE guidelines into practice: 
24 
1. Raise awareness through routine communication channels, such as email or 
25 
newsletters, regular meetings, internal staff briefings and other communications with 
26

all relevant partner organisations. Identify things staff can include in their own

4 
issues locally. 
5 
3. Carry out a baseline assessment against the recommendations to find out 
6 
whether there are gaps in current service provision. 
7 
4. Think about what data you need to measure improvement and plan how you

6. For very big changes include milestones and a business case, which will set out

Also see Leng G, Moore V, Abraham S, editors (2014) Achieving high quality care – 
1 
practical experience from NICE. Chichester: Wiley. 
2 
Context 
3 
Each year 6% of adults in England will experience an episode of depression, and 
4 
more than 15% of people will experience an episode of depression over the course 
5 
of their lifetime. For many people the episode will not be severe, but for more than 
6 
20% the depression will be more severe and have a significant impact on their daily 
7 
lives. Recurrence rates are high: there is a 50% chance of recurrence after a first 
8 
episode, rising to 70% and 90% after a second or third episode, respectively. 
9 
Women are between 1.5 and 2.5 times more likely to be diagnosed with depression 
10 
than men. However, although men are less likely to be diagnosed with depression, 
11 
they are more likely to die by suicide, have higher levels of substance misuse, and 
12 
are less likely to seek help than women. 
13 
The symptoms of depression can be disabling and the effects of the illness 
14 
pervasive. Depression can have a major detrimental effect on a person’s personal, 
15 
social and work life. This places a heavy burden on the person and their carers and 
16 
dependents, as well as placing considerable demands on the healthcare system. 
17 
Depression is expected to become the second most common cause (after ischaemic 
18 
heart disease) of loss of disability-adjusted life years in the world by 2020. 
19 
Depression is the leading cause of suicide, accounting for two-thirds of all deaths by 
20 
suicide. 
21 
Under-treatment of depression is widespread, because many people are unwilling to 
22 
seek help for depression and detection of depression by professionals is variable. 
23 
For example, of the 130 people with depression per 1,000 population, only 80 will 
24 
consult their GP. Of these 80 people, 49 are not recognised as having depression. 
25 
This is mainly because they have contacted their GP because of a somatic symptom 
26 
and do not consider themselves as having a mental health problem (despite the 
27 
presence of symptoms of depression). 
28

9 
Recommendations for research 
10 
The guideline committee has made the following recommendations for research. The 
11 
committee’s full set of research recommendations is detailed in the full guideline. 
12 
1 Effectiveness of peer support for different severities of 
13 
depression 
14 
Is peer support an effective and cost effective intervention in improving outcomes, 
15 
including symptoms, personal functioning and quality of life in adults as a stand-
16

DRAFT FOR SECOND CONSULTATION 
Depression in adults: treatment and management: NICE guideline short version DRAFT 
(May 2018) 
49 of 76 
alone intervention in people with less severe depression and as an adjunct to other 
1 
evidence based interventions in more severe depression? 
2 
Why this is important 
3 
Not all people with depression respond well to first-line treatments and for some 
4 
people the absence of good social support systems may account for the limited 
5 
response to first line interventions. A number of models for the provision of peer 
6 
support have been developed in mental health which aim to provide direct personal 
7 
support and help with establishing and maintaining supportive social networks. Peer 
8 
support is provided by people who themselves have personal experience of a mental 
9 
health problem. However, to date few studies have established and tested peer 
10 
support models for people with depression. Peer support models, including both 
11 
individual and group interventions, should be tested in a series of randomised 
12 
controlled trials which examine the effectiveness of peer support for different 
13 
severities of depression alone or in combination with evidence-based interventions 
14 
for the treatment of depression. The trials should report outcomes for a minimum of 
15 
24 months post completion of the intervention. 
16 
2 Mechanisms of action of psychological interventions 
17 
What are the mechanisms of action of effective psychological interventions for acute 
18 
episodes of depression in adults? 
19 
Why this is important 
20 
Depression is a debilitating and highly prevalent condition in adults. Despite 
21 
significant investment, the most effective and well-established treatments have only 
22 
modest effects on depressive symptoms, and the majority of treatment is for 
23 
recurrent depressive episodes. Psychological interventions are complex 
24 
interventions involving many interacting components and delivery elements. 
25 
Research is required to identify the mechanisms of action of the effective individual 
26 
psychological treatments for depression, which would allow for the isolation of the 
27 
most effective components and the development of more potent, cost-effective and 
28 
acceptable treatments. This includes examining both generic therapeutic 
29 
components (for example therapeutic relationship, rationale; remoralization), therapy 
30 
structure (for example session duration, frequency), and specific ingredients. The 
31

DRAFT FOR SECOND CONSULTATION 
Depression in adults: treatment and management: NICE guideline short version DRAFT 
(May 2018) 
50 of 76 
determination of the active components depends on testing the presence or absence 
1 
of individual therapeutic elements in rigorous study designs for example, factorial 
2 
designs. The research will need to be able to fully characterise the nature and range 
3 
of depressive symptoms experienced by people and relate these to any proposed 
4 
underlying neuropsychological mechanisms. The studies will also need to take into 
5 
account the impact of any moderators of treatment effect including therapist, patient 
6 
and environment factors. This research is necessary to improve clinical outcomes 
7 
and quality of life for patients, as well as to reduce the financial burden upon the 
8 
NHS. 
9 
3 Rate of relapse  
10 
What is the rate of relapse in people with depression who present, and are treated, 
11 
in primary and secondary care, and what factors are associated with increased risk 
12 
of relapse? 
13 
Why this is important 
14 
The current understanding of the rate of relapse in depression is that it is high and 
15 
may be up to 50% after a first episode, rising to 80% in people who have had three 
16 
or more episodes of depression. However, most studies have been undertaken in 
17 
the secondary care setting and whether these figures represent the actual rate of 
18 
relapse in primary care populations is uncertain. In addition, beyond the number of 
19 
previous episodes and the presence of residual symptoms there is also considerable 
20 
uncertainty about what other factors (biological, psychological or social) might be 
21 
associated with an increased risk of relapse. This cohort study will enable clinicians 
22 
to more accurately identify those at risk of relapse, and provide relapse prevention 
23 
strategies for these people. Accordingly, this would improve clinical outcomes and 
24 
quality of life in patients as well as facilitating more targeted use of NHS resources. 
25 
4 Group based psychological treatments for preventing relapse 
26 
What is the comparative effectiveness and cost effectiveness of group based 
27 
psychological treatments in preventing relapse in people with depression (compared 
28 
to each other and antidepressant medication) for people who have had a successful 
29 
course of treatment with antidepressants or psychological therapies? 
30

DRAFT FOR SECOND CONSULTATION 
Depression in adults: treatment and management: NICE guideline short version DRAFT 
(May 2018) 
51 of 76 
Why this is important 
1 
Depressive relapse is a frequent occurrence with implications for the wellbeing and 
2 
quality of life for the individual and financial implications for the NHS. 
3 
Antidepressants can be effective in preventing relapse but not all service users can 
4 
tolerate them or wish to take them long-term. Two, group based psychological 
5 
interventions (group CBT and mindfulness based cognitive therapy) have been 
6 
developed and shown to be effective primarily in trials when compared to treatment 
7 
as usual. However, they have not been compared with each other and only in a 
8 
limited way against antidepressants. The randomised controlled trial should be 
9 
designed to identify both moderators and mediators of treatment effect, have a 
10 
minimum follow up period of 24 months, assess any adverse events and the relative 
11 
cost-effectiveness of the interventions. 
12 
5 Increased access to services 
13 
What are the most effective and cost effective methods to promote increased access 
14 
to, and uptake of, interventions for people with depression who are under-
15 
represented in current services? 
16 
Why this is important 
17 
There is general under-recognition of depression but the problem is more marked in 
18 
certain populations. In addition, even where depression is recognised by the person 
19 
with depression or by health professionals, access to treatment can still be difficult. A 
20 
number of factors may relate to this limited access including a person’s view of their 
21 
problems, the information available on services and the location, design and systems 
22 
for referral to services. A number of studies have addressed this issue and a number 
23 
of strategies have been developed to address it but no consistent picture has 
24 
emerged from the research which can inform the design and delivery of services to 
25 
promote access. Little is also known about how these systems might be tailored to 
26 
the needs of particular groups such as older people, people from black, Asian and 
27 
minority ethnic communities, and people with disabilities who may have additional 
28 
difficulties in accessing services.   
29
